response:
The error you are encountering stems from a fundamental mismatch between your .NET 7 cross-platform architecture and the legacy dependencies required by WPF, which is strictly a Windows-only technology. WPF relies on the Windows Presentation Foundation libraries that are part of the .NET Framework ecosystem, specifically targeting version 4.0.0.0, but your application is built on .NET 7, which uses a different runtime and assembly loading mechanism that does not automatically include these older Windows-specific binaries.

To resolve this without abandoning your .NET 7 cross-platform strategy, you must isolate the WPF code into a separate class library that targets the .NET Framework or .NET 7 with Windows-specific conditions, ensuring it is only compiled and loaded when the operating system is detected as Windows. You can achieve this by using conditional compilation symbols or by creating a distinct project structure where the WPF window logic resides in a project that is excluded from the build process on macOS, effectively preventing the runtime from attempting to load the missing PresentationFramework assembly on non-Windows platforms.

cousin to the approach of conditional compilation is the use of dependency injection to abstract the window creation logic behind a common interface that your main application can call regardless of the underlying OS. By defining an interface in your shared code that declares methods for opening windows, you can implement this interface differently for Windows using the WPF window and for macOS using a Cocoa-based window, ensuring that the WPF-specific assembly is never referenced or loaded when the application runs on a Mac.

When configuring your solution, ensure that the WPF project is set to target a framework that supports WPF, such as net6.0-windows or net48, and that your main .NET 7 project references this WPF project only conditionally based on the target platform. This setup allows the build system to skip the WPF project entirely during the macOS build, eliminating the file not found error at runtime because the dependency simply does not exist in the compiled output for that platform.

Finally, verify that your project files contain the appropriate conditional imports and that you are not inadvertently including WPF assemblies in your shared or netstandard libraries, as this would force the runtime to search for them on all platforms. By strictly compartmentalizing the Windows-specific UI code and using platform detection to route the execution flow, you can maintain a single codebase that compiles and runs efficiently on both Windows and macOS without triggering assembly loading errors.
